1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt, saying," The witnesses note the LORD speaking from the tabernacle in the wilderness of Sinai — the crux of God ordering his people from his dwelling. On "the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt," interpreters expound the opening of Numbers. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the setting — "the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation": the book opens with God speaking to Moses from the tabernacle now reared up, in the wilderness, to order and number his people; the witnesses note the significance — the precise time and place (the wilderness of Sinai, the tabernacle of the congregation, the first day of the second month of the second year, a month after the tabernacle was set up, Exodus 40:17); the lesson — God speaking from the tabernacle, the place of his dwelling and his oracle (the voice from the mercy seat, the God who dwells among his people now ordering them, Numbers 7:89); the figure — the God of order who numbers and arranges his people for the journey, the host of the LORD set in array; the careful dating marking the historical reality and the new stage, the people now to be mustered and marshalled for the march to Canaan; the figure of God who dwells among his people and orders all their goings, the tabernacle the center from which he commands.

On "in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the place. Keil and Ellicott note the wilderness and the tabernacle (God's dwelling now reared, the place of the oracle, Numbers 7:89) — the setting (God speaking from his dwelling).

On "on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the date. Wesley and Gill note the precise dating (a month after the tabernacle was set up, Exodus 40:17) — the figure (the new stage, the people ordered for the march); the providence (God ordering his people from the tabernacle). The verse displays the LORD speaking from the tabernacle in the wilderness of Sinai.

The witnesses agree the substance: God spoke to Moses from the tabernacle in the wilderness, in the second year, to order his people (Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God ordering his people from his dwelling: the LORD speaking from the tabernacle — the God who dwells among his people now speaking from his sanctuary to muster and marshal them (Numbers 7:89), the careful dating marking the new stage of the march to Canaan (Exodus 40:17), the God of order who numbers and arranges his host. The verse displays the divine-ordering crux. The LORD speaking from the tabernacle in the wilderness of Sinai displays the crux: the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tabernacle of the congregation (Numbers 7:89; Exodus 40:17) — the God who dwells among his people speaking from his sanctuary to order and number them, the God of order who marshals his host for the journey. The verse displays the command to number the congregation (vv.2-3).
